questions.question: If A = {1,2,3,4} and B = {3,4,5,6}, then A ■ B (symmetric difference) is:
The number of subsets of a set with n elements is:
questions.hint: Every element has 2 choices (in or out). Total subsets = 2■. For n=3: 2³=8 subsets.

questions.question: If n(A)=3, n(B)=4 and n(A∩B)=2, then n(A∪B) is:
A relation R on set A is called an equivalence relation if it is:
questions.hint: Equivalence relation must satisfy all three: reflexive, symmetric AND transitive.

questions.question: Which of the following functions is one-one (injective)?
questions.hint: f(x)=2x+3 is strictly increasing → one-one. Others fail: f(x)=f(-x) for x² and |x|.

questions.question: The domain of f(x) = √(4–x²) is:
questions.hint: Need 4–x² ≥ 0 → x² ≤ 4 → –2 ≤ x ≤ 2. Endpoints included (square root of 0 is 0).

questions.question: If f(x) = x² and g(x) = √x, then fog(x) is:
questions.hint: fog(x) = f(g(x)) = f(√x) = (√x)² = x, for x ≥ 0.

questions.question: The inverse function of f(x) = (x–1)/(x+1) is:
questions.hint: Let y=(x–1)/(x+1). Swap x,y and solve: x(y+1)=y–1 → y=(1+x)/(1–x).

questions.question: If A is a 3×3 matrix and |A| = 5, then |3A| is:
questions.hint: |kA| = k■|A| for an n×n matrix. |3A| = 3³×5 = 27×5 = 135.

questions.question: A square matrix A is singular if:
questions.hint: Singular matrix: |A|=0, no inverse exists. Non-singular: |A|≠0, inverse exists.

questions.question: The value of the determinant |1 2 3 / 4 5 6 / 7 8 9| is:
questions.hint: R3 = R1+R2 (7=1+4? No). Actually R3–R2=R2–R1 (AP rows). Rows in AP → det=0.

questions.question: If A is symmetric, then A■ equals:
questions.hint: Symmetric matrix: A■ = A. Skew-symmetric: A■ = –A.

questions.question: The rank of the zero matrix (all entries = 0) is:
questions.hint:
Rank = number of non-zero rows in row echelon form. Zero matrix has no non-zero
rows → rank 0.

questions.question: If AB = BA, then matrices A and B are said to be:
questions.hint: Commutative property of multiplication. Generally AB ≠ BA for matrices.

questions.question: For any square matrix A, A + A■ is always:
questions.hint: (A+A■)■ = A■+A = A+A■. So it equals its own transpose → symmetric.

questions.question: The cofactor C■■ of matrix A = [[1,2],[3,4]] is:
If A² = A, the matrix A is called:
questions.hint: A² = A → idempotent. A² = I → involutory. A■ = 0 → nilpotent. AA■ = I → orthogonal.

questions.question: The modulus of z = 3 + 4i is:
The argument of z = –1 + i√3 is:
questions.hint: z is in 2nd quadrant. tan■¹(√3/1)=π/3. arg = π–π/3 = 2π/3.

questions.question: i■■ equals:
questions.hint: Powers of i cycle with period 4. 47 = 4×11+3. i■■ = i³ = –i.

questions.question: The conjugate of z = (2+3i)/(1–i) is:
questions.hint:
Multiply num & denom by (1+i): (2+3i)(1+i)/2 = (2+2i+3i–3)/2 = (–1+5i)/2. Conjugate:
(–1–5i)/2.

questions.question: De Moivre's theorem: (cosθ + i sinθ)■ equals:
options: B) n cosθ + n i sinθ
questions.hint:
De Moivre's: (cisθ)■ = cis(nθ) = cos(nθ)+i sin(nθ). Fundamental to complex number
theory.

questions.question: The cube roots of unity are 1, ω, ω². Then 1 + ω + ω² equals:
questions.hint: Sum of nth roots of unity = 0. So 1+ω+ω²=0. Also ω³=1 and ω≠1.

questions.question: If z = r(cosθ + i sinθ), then z■ (conjugate) in polar form is:
questions.hint: Conjugate negates imaginary part: z■ = r(cosθ – i sinθ) = r·cis(–θ).

questions.question: The number of solutions of z² + |z| = 0 (z complex) is:
questions.hint:
z=0 is one solution. For z≠0 write z=re^(iθ): r²e^(2iθ)+r=0 → r=1 (→ e^(2iθ)=–1 →
θ=π/2,3π/2). Total: 3.

questions.question: If α, β are roots of 2x²–5x+3=0, then α+β and αβ are:
questions.hint: By Vieta: α+β = –b/a = 5/2; αβ = c/a = 3/2.

questions.question: The discriminant of ax²+bx+c=0 for real and distinct roots must be:
questions.hint: Δ=b²–4ac. Δ>0: 2 real distinct roots; Δ=0: equal roots; Δ<0: complex roots.

questions.question: The equation whose roots are 2α and 2β (where α,β are roots of x²–5x+6=0) is:
questions.hint: α+β=5, αβ=6. New roots: 2α+2β=10, 2α·2β=4·6=24. Equation: x²–10x+24=0.

questions.question: If one root of x²–px+q=0 is twice the other, then:
questions.hint: Roots α,2α. α+2α=3α=p→α=p/3. α·2α=2α²=q→2(p/3)²=q→2p²/9=q→2p²=9q.

questions.question: The maximum value of f(x) = –x² + 4x – 3 is:
questions.hint: Vertex at x=–b/2a=–4/(–2)=2. f(2)=–4+8–3=1. For a<0, vertex gives maximum.

questions.question: The number of real roots of x² + x + 1 = 0 is:
questions.hint: Δ = 1–4 = –3 < 0. No real roots. Both roots are complex conjugates.

questions.question: If α+β=–2 and αβ=–3, the quadratic equation is:
The equation x■–5x²+4=0 has how many real solutions?
questions.hint: Let y=x²: y²–5y+4=0→(y–1)(y–4)=0→y=1,4→x=±1,±2. Four real solutions.

questions.question: The value of ¹■C■ is:
The number of ways to arrange the letters of the word MISSISSIPPI is:
In how many ways can 5 boys and 3 girls be seated in a row so that no two girls are adjacent?
questions.hint:
First arrange 5 boys: 5! = 120 ways. 6 gaps (including ends). Choose 3 of 6 for girls:
6P3=120. Total=120×120=14400.

questions.question: The number of diagonals in a polygon of n sides is:
questions.hint:
Total diagonals = ■C■ – n = n(n–1)/2 – n = n(n–3)/2. (Subtract n sides from all
connections)

questions.question: How many 4-digit numbers can be formed using digits 1–9 without repetition?
questions.hint: 9P4 = 9×8×7×6 = 3024. (Permutation: order matters, no repetition)

questions.question: The number of ways to select a committee of 3 from 10 people is:
questions.hint: ¹■C■ = 120 (combination, order doesn't matter in committee selection).

questions.question: The number of circular arrangements of 6 distinct objects is:
questions.hint: Circular permutations = (n–1)! = 5! = 120. Also 6!/6 = 720/6 = 120.

questions.question: The middle term in the expansion of (x+y)■ is:
questions.hint: For even n, middle term is T_(n/2+1) = T_(8/2+1) = T■ (the 5th term, r=4).

questions.question: The coefficient of x³ in the expansion of (1+x)■ is:
questions.hint: Coefficient of x³ = ■C■ = 5!/(3!2!) = 10.

questions.question: The sum of coefficients in (1+x)■ is obtained by putting x=1 and equals:
questions.hint: Sum of all coefficients: put x=1 in (1+x)■ = 2■. (Sum of ■C■+■C■+...+■C■ = 2■)

questions.question: In the expansion of (2x – 1/x)¹², the term independent of x is the:
The number of terms in the expansion of (a+b+c)■ is:
questions.hint: Number of terms in (a+b+c)■ = ■■²C■ = (n+1)(n+2)/2.

questions.question: The 10th term of AP: 3, 7, 11, 15, … is:
Sum of first n terms of AP is S_n = n/2 × (2a + (n–1)d). Sum of 1+3+5+…to 20 terms is:
questions.hint:
a=1, d=2, n=20. S■■=20/2×(2+19×2)=10×40=400. (Also sum of first n odd numbers =
n²=20²=400)

questions.question: The 5th term of GP: 2, 6, 18, 54, … is:
Sum of infinite GP with a=5, r=1/2 is:
AM of two numbers is 5 and GM is 4. The two numbers are:
If a, b, c are in HP, then b is the HM of a and c. The relation is:
Sum of squares of first n natural numbers: 1²+2²+…+n² equals:
The series 1 + 1/2! + 1/3! + 1/4! + … converges to:
The value of sin 75° is:
The general solution of sinθ = sinα is:
questions.hint: sinθ=sinα → θ=nπ+(–1)■α. For cosθ=cosα: θ=2nπ±α. For tanθ=tanα: θ=nπ+α.

questions.question: sin(A+B)·sin(A–B) equals:
questions.hint:
sin(A+B)sin(A–B)=(sinAcosB+cosAsinB)(sinAcosB–cosAsinB)=sin²Acos²B–cos²Asin²B
=sin²A–sin²B=cos²B–cos²A.

questions.question: The value of cos 20° + cos 100° + cos 140° is:
questions.hint:
These are three cos values equally spaced by 60°. Sum of cos of angles in AP with
sum 360° → sum = 0.

questions.question: tan(A+B) = (tanA + tanB)/(1 – tanA·tanB). If tanA = 1/3 and tanB = 1/4, then tan(A+B) is:
2sin A cos B = ?
questions.hint: Product to sum: 2sinAcosB = sin(A+B)+sin(A–B). (Important identity for EAPCET!)

questions.question: The period of f(x) = tan(3x) is:
questions.hint: Period of tan(nx) = π/n. Period of tan(3x) = π/3.

questions.question: If sin A = 3/5 and A is in 1st quadrant, then cos 2A is:
The maximum value of 3sinx + 4cosx is:
questions.hint: Max of asinx+bcosx = √(a²+b²) = √(9+16) = √25 = 5.

questions.question: sin 3A in terms of sin A is:
questions.hint:
sin3A=3sinA–4sin³A. Similarly cos3A=4cos³A–3cosA. These are standard cubic
identities.

questions.question: The principal value of sin■¹(1/2) is:
questions.hint: sin(π/6)=1/2 and π/6 ∈ [–π/2, π/2] (principal range of sin■¹). So sin■¹(1/2)=π/6.

questions.question: tan■¹(1) + tan■¹(2) + tan■¹(3) equals:
sin■¹x + cos■¹x equals (for x∈[–1,1]):
questions.hint: sin■¹x + cos■¹x = π/2 for all x ∈ [–1,1]. (Standard identity to remember!)

questions.question: The domain of cos■¹(2x–1) is:
2tan■¹(1/3) + tan■¹(1/7) equals:
questions.hint:
tan■¹(1/3)+tan■¹(1/3)=tan■¹(3/4) (using formula).
tan■¹(3/4)+tan■¹(1/7)=tan■¹(1)=π/4.

questions.question: The value of tan(sin■¹(3/5)) is:
questions.hint: sin■¹(3/5)=θ means sinθ=3/5. Then cosθ=4/5 (Pythagorean). tanθ=sinθ/cosθ=3/4.

questions.question: In triangle ABC, by the sine rule, a/sinA = b/sinB = c/sinC equals:
questions.hint: Sine rule: a/sinA = b/sinB = c/sinC = 2R, where R is circumradius.

questions.question: By cosine rule in ΔABC: cos A equals:
questions.hint: Cosine rule: a²=b²+c²–2bc cosA → cosA=(b²+c²–a²)/2bc.

questions.question: Area of triangle ABC = ½ ab sinC. If a=5, b=6, C=30°, the area is:
questions.hint: Area = ½×5×6×sin30° = ½×30×(1/2) = 7.5 sq units.

questions.question: The inradius r of a triangle with area Δ and semi-perimeter s is:
questions.hint: Inradius r = Area/semi-perimeter = Δ/s. (Circumradius R = abc/4Δ)

questions.question: In ΔABC, if A=60°, B=75°, then C equals:
In triangle with sides 3, 4, 5; the triangle is:
questions.hint: 3²+4²=9+16=25=5². Satisfies Pythagoras theorem → right-angled triangle.

questions.question: The formula for tan(A/2) in terms of sides is:
questions.hint: tan(A/2)=√[(s–b)(s–c)/{s(s–a)}]. Also r=(s–a)tan(A/2).

questions.question: In equilateral triangle of side a, the circumradius R is:
questions.hint: For equilateral: R = a/√3. (Using R=abc/4Δ: a³/(4×√3a²/4)=a³/(√3a²)=a/√3)

questions.question: The slope of a line passing through (2,3) and (5,7) is:
The equation of line with slope 2 passing through (1,3) is:
Distance from point (3,4) to line 4x–3y+5=0 is:
Two lines a■x+b■y+c■=0 and a■x+b■y+c■=0 are parallel if:
questions.hint: Parallel: a■/a■=b■/b■≠c■/c■ → a■b■=a■b■. Perpendicular: a■a■+b■b■=0.

questions.question: The angle between lines y = m■x+c■ and y = m■x+c■ is given by tan θ =:
questions.hint: tan θ = |(m■–m■)/(1+m■m■)|. Lines are perpendicular if m■m■=–1.

questions.question: The x-intercept of line 3x+4y–12=0 is:
questions.hint: Put y=0: 3x=12→x=4. x-intercept=4. Put x=0: 4y=12→y=3. y-intercept=3.

questions.question: The equation of the pair of lines ax²+2hxy+by²=0 represents two perpendicular lines if:
questions.hint:
Pair of lines ax²+2hxy+by²=0 is perpendicular if coefficient of x² + coefficient of y² = 0,
i.e., a+b=0.

questions.question: The angle bisectors of ax²+2hxy+by²=0 are given by:
questions.hint: Angle bisectors: (x²–y²)/(a–b)=xy/h, equivalent to hx²–(a–b)xy–hy²=0.

questions.question: The area of the triangle formed by the axes and line x/a + y/b = 1 is:
questions.hint: Intercept form: x-intercept=a, y-intercept=b. Area=½|base×height|=½|a||b|=|ab|/2.

questions.question: The image of point (1,2) with respect to line y=x is:
questions.hint: Reflection in y=x: (x,y)→(y,x). Image of (1,2) is (2,1).

questions.question: The general equation of a circle is x²+y²+2gx+2fy+c=0. Its centre and radius are:
questions.hint: Centre=(–g,–f), radius=√(g²+f²–c). For real circle: g²+f²–c>0.

questions.question: The length of tangent from external point (x■,y■) to circle x²+y²+2gx+2fy+c=0 is:
questions.hint: Length of tangent = √(S■) where S■=x■²+y■²+2gx■+2fy■+c.

questions.question: The equation of circle with centre (2,–3) and radius 5 is:
Two circles intersect orthogonally if:
questions.hint:
Orthogonal circles: angle between tangents at intersection=90°. Condition:
2g■g■+2f■f■=c■+c■.

questions.question: The equation of the tangent to circle x²+y²=r² at point (x■,y■) is:
questions.hint: Equation of tangent at (x■,y■): T=0 → xx■+yy■=r².

questions.question: The common chord of circles x²+y²=16 and (x–2)²+y²=16 lies on the line:
questions.hint: Subtract equations: x²+y²–16 – [(x–2)²+y²–16]=0 → x²–(x²–4x+4)=0 → 4x–4=0 → x=1.

questions.question: The number of tangents that can be drawn from an external point to a circle is:
questions.hint: From external point: exactly 2 tangents. From point on circle: 1. From interior: 0.

questions.question: The radical axis of two circles is:
options: B) A straight line perpendicular to the line of centres
questions.explanation:
A straight line perpendicular
to the line of centres
questions.hint:
Radical axis (locus of equal tangent lengths) ⊥ to line joining centres. Obtained by
subtracting circle equations.

questions.question: The focus of parabola y² = 4ax is:
questions.hint: y²=4ax: focus=(a,0), directrix x=–a, vertex=(0,0), axis=x-axis, latus rectum=4a.

questions.question: The eccentricity of an ellipse x²/a²+y²/b²=1 (a>b) is:
questions.hint: For ellipse: c²=a²–b², e=c/a < 1. For hyperbola: c²=a²+b², e=c/a > 1.

questions.question: The equation of directrix of parabola y² = 12x is:
questions.hint: y²=12x → 4a=12 → a=3. Directrix: x=–a=–3.

questions.question: The sum of the focal distances of any point on the ellipse x²/a²+y²/b²=1 is:
questions.hint:
Sum of focal distances = 2a (constant). This is the definition of ellipse. For any point P:
PF■+PF■=2a.

questions.question: The foci of ellipse x²/25 + y²/16 = 1 are:
questions.hint: a²=25, b²=16. c²=a²–b²=9 → c=3. Foci: (±3,0) on major axis (x-axis).

questions.question: The asymptotes of hyperbola x²/a²–y²/b²=1 are:
questions.hint: Asymptotes of x²/a²–y²/b²=1 are y=(b/a)x and y=–(b/a)x. They pass through centre.

questions.question: The length of latus rectum of ellipse x²/a²+y²/b²=1 (a>b) is:
questions.hint:
LR of ellipse = 2b²/a. LR of parabola y²=4ax is 4a. LR of hyperbola x²/a²–y²/b²=1 is
2b²/a.

questions.question: The rectangular hyperbola xy = c² has eccentricity:
questions.hint:
Rectangular hyperbola has asymptotes perpendicular to each other. Its eccentricity =
√2.

questions.question: The parametric equations of parabola y²=4ax are:
questions.hint:
Para: (at²,2at). Ellipse: (acosθ,bsinθ). Hyperbola: (asecθ,btanθ). These are standard
parametric forms.

questions.question: The equation x² – y² = a² represents:
questions.hint:
x²/a²–y²/a²=1 is hyperbola with a=b→rectangular hyperbola. Asymptotes: y=±x
(perpendicular).

questions.question: The distance between points A(1,2,3) and B(4,6,3) is:
Direction cosines of vector 2î+3■+6k■ are:
The equation of a plane passing through point (1,2,3) with normal vector (2,3,4) is:
The dot product a■·b■ = |a||b|cosθ. For perpendicular vectors, a■·b■ equals:
questions.hint: Perpendicular (θ=90°): cosθ=0 → a■·b■=0. This is the condition for orthogonality.

questions.question: The cross product |a■×b■| = |a||b|sinθ. For parallel vectors, |a■×b■| equals:
questions.hint:
Parallel (θ=0° or 180°): sinθ=0 → |a■×b■|=0. (Cross product of parallel vectors is zero
vector)

questions.question: The volume of parallelepiped with vectors a■, b■, c■ is:
questions.hint: Volume = |[a■ b■ c■]| = |a■·(b■×c■)| = scalar triple product.

questions.question: The angle between planes 2x+y–2z=5 and 3x–6y–2z=7 is:
If |a■|=3, |b■|=4 and angle between them is 60°, then |a■+b■| is:
The vector projection of a■ on b■ is:
questions.hint:
Scalar projection of a■ on b■ = a■·b■ = (a■·b■)/|b■|. Vector projection =
[(a■·b■)/|b■|²]b■.

questions.question: The foot of perpendicular from point A(1,0,0) to the line x/1=y/0=z/0 is:
questions.hint: The line is x-axis (y=0,z=0). Foot of perpendicular from (1,0,0) to x-axis is (1,0,0) itself.

questions.question: The shortest distance between skew lines r■=a■+λb■ and r■=c■+µd■ is:
questions.hint:
SD = |(c■–a■)·(b■×d■)|/|b■×d■|. For intersecting lines SD=0; for parallel lines use
different formula.

questions.question: The vectors a■ = 2î+3■–k■ and b■ = 4î+6■–2k■ are:
questions.hint:
b■=2a■ (each component of b is twice that of a). Parallel means one is scalar multiple
of other.

questions.question: lim(x→0) sinx/x equals:
questions.hint: Standard limit: lim(x→0) sinx/x = 1 (x in radians). For degrees: limit = π/180.

questions.question: lim(x→0) (e■ – 1)/x equals:
questions.hint: lim(x→0)(e■–1)/x=1. Similarly lim(x→0)(a■–1)/x=lna. Standard exponential limits.

questions.question: lim(x→∞) (1+1/x)■ equals:
questions.hint: lim(x→∞)(1+1/x)■ = e ≈ 2.718... This is the definition of Euler's number e.

questions.question: lim(x→2) (x²–4)/(x–2) equals:
A function f(x) is continuous at x=a if:
questions.hint: Continuity requires: (1) f(a) defined, (2) limit exists, (3) limit equals f(a).

questions.question: lim(x→0) (log(1+x))/x equals:
questions.hint: lim(x→0) ln(1+x)/x = 1. (Using L'Hopital: d/dx[ln(1+x)] at x=0 = 1/(1+0)=1; d/dx[x]=1)

questions.question: The function f(x) = 1/x is discontinuous at x =
questions.hint: f(x)=1/x is not defined at x=0. Both one-sided limits are ±∞. Not removable.

questions.question: lim(x→0) (tanx – sinx)/x³ equals:
questions.hint:
tanx–sinx = sinx(1/cosx–1)=sinx(1–cosx)/cosx. Near 0: sinx≈x, 1–cosx≈x²/2, cosx≈1.
Limit→(x·x²/2)/x³=1/2.

questions.question: d/dx(x■) equals:
questions.hint: Power rule: d/dx(x■) = nx■■¹. Fundamental rule of differentiation.

questions.question: d/dx(sin x) = cosx. Using chain rule, d/dx(sin(x²)) equals:
questions.hint: d/dx[sin(x²)] = cos(x²)·d/dx(x²) = cos(x²)·2x = 2x cos(x²).

questions.question: The derivative of log■x is:
questions.hint: d/dx(ln x) = 1/x. For d/dx(log_a x) = 1/(x lna).

questions.question: The derivative of e■ is:
questions.hint: d/dx(e■) = e■. The exponential function is its own derivative.

questions.question: If y = x² sin x, then dy/dx is (product rule):
questions.hint: Product rule: (uv)' = u'v+uv'. d/dx(x²·sinx)=2x·sinx+x²·cosx.

questions.question: If y=f(x)/g(x), then dy/dx by quotient rule is:
questions.hint:
Quotient rule: d/dx(f/g)=(f'g–fg')/g². Numerator: derivative of top × bottom – top ×
derivative of bottom.

questions.question: The derivative of tan■¹x is:
questions.hint: d/dx(tan■¹x)=1/(1+x²). d/dx(sin■¹x)=1/√(1–x²). d/dx(cos■¹x)=–1/√(1–x²).

questions.question: Second derivative of y=sin x is:
questions.hint: y'=cosx, y''=d/dx(cosx)=–sinx. Note: y''''=y (4th derivative of sinx = sinx).

questions.question: If x=at², y=2at (parametric), then dy/dx is:
questions.hint: dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt) = 2a/(2at) = 1/t.

questions.question: Rolle's theorem requires: f continuous on [a,b], differentiable on (a,b) and:
questions.hint: Rolle's theorem: if f(a)=f(b), then ∃c∈(a,b) s.t. f'(c)=0. (Special case of MVT)

questions.question: The condition for f(x) to have a local minimum at x=c is:
questions.hint:
f'(c)=0 (critical point). f''(c)>0 → concave up → local min. f''(c)<0 → concave down →
local max.

questions.question: The equation of tangent to y=x² at point (2,4) is:
questions.hint: f'(x)=2x. Slope at (2,4)=4. Tangent: y–4=4(x–2) → y=4x–4.

questions.question: The slope of normal to curve y=x² at (1,1) is:
questions.hint: f'(1)=2 (slope of tangent). Normal slope = –1/f'(1) = –1/2.

questions.question: A spherical balloon is inflated at 100 cm³/s. Rate of increase of radius when r=5 cm is:
questions.hint: V=4πr³/3. dV/dt=4πr²(dr/dt). 100=4π(25)(dr/dt) → dr/dt=100/100π=1/π cm/s.

questions.question: The function f(x)=2x³–3x²–12x+4 is increasing in:
The maximum area of rectangle inscribed in a circle of radius r is:
questions.hint:
For rectangle in circle of radius r, if diagonal=2r, max area occurs at square: side=r√2.
Area=2r².

questions.question: By LMVT, if f(x)=x² on [1,3], the value of c is:
The approximate value of √26 using differentiation (near √25=5) is:
∫x■ dx equals (n ≠ –1):
questions.hint:
Power rule for integration: ∫x■dx = x■■¹/(n+1)+C (add 1 to power, divide by new
power).

questions.question: ∫(1/x)dx equals:
questions.hint: ∫(1/x)dx = ln|x|+C. This is the case n=–1 excluded from power rule.

questions.question: ∫e■dx equals:
questions.hint: ∫e■dx = e■+C. ∫a■dx = a■/lna+C. ∫e^(ax)dx=e^(ax)/a+C.

questions.question: ∫sin x dx equals:
questions.hint: ∫sinx dx = –cosx+C. ∫cosx dx = sinx+C. (Opposite of differentiating)

questions.question: The value of ∫■^π sin x dx is:
∫x e■ dx (by integration by parts) equals:
questions.hint: ∫xe■dx: u=x, dv=e■dx. =[xe■–∫e■dx]=xe■–e■+C=e■(x–1)+C.

questions.question: The area under curve y=x² from x=0 to x=3 is:
questions.hint: ∫■³x²dx=[x³/3]■³=27/3–0=9 sq units.

questions.question: ∫■■¹ x³ dx equals:
questions.hint: x³ is an odd function. ∫■■■ f(x)dx=0 for odd f. (Directly: [x■/4]■■¹=1/4–1/4=0.)

questions.question: Order of differential equation d²y/dx² + 3(dy/dx)³ + y = 0 is:
questions.hint:
Order = order of highest derivative = 2 (d²y/dx²). Degree = power of highest order
derivative = 1.

questions.question: Solution of dy/dx = y gives:
questions.hint: Separate variables: dy/y = dx. Integrate: lny=x+k → y=e^(x+k)=Ce■.

questions.question: The integrating factor of dy/dx + Py = Q is:
questions.hint: For linear ODE dy/dx+Py=Q: IF = e^(∫Pdx). Solution: y×IF = ∫Q×IF dx + C.

questions.question: The degree of (dy/dx)² + y = x is:
questions.hint:
Degree = power of highest order derivative when equation is polynomial in derivatives.
Here degree=2.

questions.question: Two dice are thrown. Probability that sum is 7 is:
Bayes' theorem: P(A|B) = P(B|A)·P(A)/P(B). This relates to:
questions.hint:
Bayes' theorem is used for conditional probability: updating probability given new
evidence.

 Best of luck for EAPCET 2026 — You are going to ace it! ■

questions.question: The dimensional formula of angular momentum is:
questions.hint: L = mvr = [M][LT■¹][L] = [ML²T■¹]. Also L = Iω = [ML²][T■¹] = [ML²T■¹]

questions.question: The percentage error in measurement of radius is 2%. The percentage error in volume of sphere is:
questions.hint: V = (4/3)πr³. % error in V = 3 × (% error in r) = 3 × 2% = 6%

questions.question: Which of the following pairs has the same dimensional formula?
questions.hint: Torque τ = F×r = [ML²T■²]; Work W = F·d = [ML²T■²]. Same dimensions!

questions.question: Light year is a unit of:
questions.hint: 1 light year = distance light travels in one year ≈ 9.46 × 10¹■ m.

questions.question: A body is thrown vertically upward with velocity u. Time to reach maximum height is:
questions.hint: At max height, v = 0. Using v = u - gt → 0 = u - gt → t = u/g

questions.question: A projectile is fired at angle θ with horizontal. The horizontal range is maximum when θ equals:
questions.hint: R = u²sin2θ/g. R is maximum when sin2θ = 1 → 2θ = 90° → θ = 45°

questions.question: Velocity-time graph of uniform acceleration is a:
questions.hint: v = u + at. This is linear in t → v-t graph is a straight line with slope = a.

questions.question: A ball is dropped from height h. Time to reach ground is:
questions.hint: h = ½gt² → t = √(2h/g). Initial velocity u = 0 for a dropped body.

questions.question: For a particle moving in a circle of radius r with uniform speed v, centripetal acceleration is:
questions.hint: Centripetal acceleration a = v²/r = ω²r. Directed towards centre.

questions.question: A body of mass 5 kg is acted upon by a net force of 20 N. Its acceleration is:
questions.hint: Newton's 2nd law: F = ma → a = F/m = 20/5 = 4 m/s²

questions.question:
A body of mass 2 kg moving at 10 m/s collides and sticks to a body of mass 3 kg at rest. Their
combined velocity is:
questions.hint: Conservation of momentum: m■u■ = (m■+m■)v → 2×10 = 5×v → v = 4 m/s

questions.question:
The coefficient of static friction between a block and surface is 0.4 (g=10 m/s²). Maximum static friction
on a 5 kg block is:
Which of the following is a consequence of Newton's Third Law?
questions.hint:
Newton's 3rd law (action-reaction) leads directly to conservation of linear
momentum.

questions.question: A 60 kg person stands in a lift accelerating upward at 2 m/s². Apparent weight (g=10 m/s²) is:
questions.hint: Apparent weight = m(g+a) = 60(10+2) = 60×12 = 720 N

questions.question: The work done by a force F = 5 N on a body through displacement d = 4 m at 60° is:
The kinetic energy of a body of mass 2 kg moving at 6 m/s is:
questions.hint: KE = ½mv² = ½ × 2 × 6² = ½ × 2 × 36 = 36 J

questions.question: A spring of force constant k is compressed by x. The elastic potential energy stored is:
questions.hint:
Elastic PE = ½kx². This energy is released when spring returns to natural
length.

questions.question: A machine does 1000 J of work in 10 s. Its power output is:
In an elastic collision:
options: C) Both momentum and KE are conserved
questions.explanation:
Both momentum
and KE are conserved
questions.hint:
Elastic collision: both linear momentum AND kinetic energy are conserved.
Coefficient of restitution = 1.

questions.question: The moment of inertia of a solid disc of mass M and radius R about its central axis is:
questions.hint: I(disc) = ½MR². For ring I = MR², for solid sphere I = 2/5 MR².

questions.question: Angular momentum L = Iω. If I is halved and angular momentum is conserved, ω becomes:
questions.hint: L = Iω = constant. If I → I/2, then ω → 2ω (like ice skater pulling in arms).

questions.question: Torque τ = r × F. The SI unit of torque is:
questions.hint:
τ = r × F → unit = m × N = N·m. Although dimensions equal Joule, torque is
expressed in N·m.

questions.question: A body rolls without slipping. The ratio of rotational KE to total KE for a solid sphere is:
The theorem of parallel axes states that I = Icm + Md². Here d is:
options: B) Distance between the two parallel axes
C) Distance from centre of mass to surface
questions.explanation:
Distance between
the two parallel axes
questions.hint:
Parallel axis theorem: I = Icm + Md² where d = distance between parallel axis
and CM axis.

questions.question: The orbital velocity of a satellite close to Earth's surface (R = 6400 km, g = 9.8 m/s²) is approximately:
Kepler's third law states that T² ∝ r³. If the orbital radius is doubled, the time period becomes:
Escape velocity from Earth's surface is:
questions.hint: v_esc = √(2GM/R) = √(2gR) ≈ 11.2 km/s for Earth.

questions.question: The gravitational potential energy of a body of mass m at height h above Earth (radius R) is:
questions.hint: U = -GMm/r where r = R+h from Earth's centre. Negative sign: bound system.

questions.question: Young's modulus is defined as:
questions.hint: Young's modulus Y = (F/A)/(ΔL/L) = Longitudinal stress / Longitudinal strain.

questions.question: Surface tension has dimensions:
questions.hint: T = F/L = [MLT■²]/[L] = [MT■²]. Unit = N/m.

questions.question: Bernoulli's theorem is based on conservation of:
questions.hint:
Bernoulli's equation: P + ½ρv² + ρgh = constant → conservation of energy per
unit volume.

questions.question: The excess pressure inside a soap bubble of radius R and surface tension T is:
questions.hint:
Soap bubble has 2 surfaces. Excess pressure = 4T/R. For liquid drop: 2T/R (1
surface).

questions.question: For an ideal gas at temperature T, the rms speed of molecules is proportional to:
questions.hint: v_rms = √(3RT/M) ∝ √T. Doubling T increases v_rms by factor √2.

questions.question: For a monoatomic ideal gas, the ratio γ = Cp/Cv is:
questions.hint: Monoatomic gas: f=3. Cv = 3R/2, Cp = 5R/2. γ = Cp/Cv = 5/3 ≈ 1.67

questions.question: Newton's law of cooling states that the rate of cooling is proportional to:
options: C) Difference in temperatures of body and surroundings
questions.explanation:
Difference in
temperatures of body and
surroundings
questions.hint:
dQ/dt ∝ (T - T■). Rate of cooling depends on temperature excess over
surroundings.

questions.question: The efficiency of a Carnot engine operating between 300 K and 500 K is:
Wien's displacement law states that λ_max × T = constant. If T is doubled, λ_max:
questions.hint: λ_max ∝ 1/T. If T → 2T, then λ_max → λ_max/2.

questions.question: The time period of a simple pendulum of length L is:
questions.hint:
T = 2π√(L/g). Time period is independent of mass and amplitude (for small
oscillations).

questions.question: For SHM x = A sinωt, the maximum velocity is:
questions.hint:
v = dx/dt = Aω cosωt. Maximum when cosωt = 1 → v_max = Aω (at mean
position).

questions.question: In SHM, at mean position the restoring force is:
questions.hint: F = -kx. At mean position x = 0 → F = 0. Velocity is maximum here.

questions.question: The time period of a spring-mass system (mass m, spring constant k) is:
questions.hint:
T = 2π√(m/k). Unlike pendulum, it depends on mass but is independent of g and
amplitude.

questions.question: The speed of sound in air at 0°C is 332 m/s. At 4°C it is approximately:
questions.hint:
v ∝ √T. v = 332√(277/273) ≈ 333.2 m/s. Speed of sound increases with
temperature.

questions.question: Doppler effect: A source moves towards a stationary observer. The observed frequency:
questions.hint:
When source approaches observer, wavefronts are compressed → shorter
wavelength → higher frequency.

questions.question: Beats are produced due to:
options: A) Interference of two waves of same frequency

B) Superposition of two waves of slightly different
frequencies
questions.explanation:
Superposition of
two waves of slightly different
frequencies
questions.hint:
Beat frequency = |f■ - f■|. Beats are periodic variations in loudness due to
interference.

questions.question: For a standing wave, nodes are points of:
questions.hint:
Nodes: zero displacement (max pressure variation). Antinodes: max
displacement (min pressure variation).

questions.question: The frequency of the fundamental mode of vibration of an open pipe of length L is:
questions.hint:
Open pipe: both ends antinodes. Fundamental: L = λ/2 → f = v/2L (closed:
v/4L).

questions.question:
Coulomb's law: Force between charges q■ and q■ at distance r is F = kq■q■/r². If distance is tripled,
force becomes:
questions.hint: F ∝ 1/r². If r → 3r, F → F/9. Force decreases by factor 9.

questions.question: Electric potential at distance r from charge q is:
questions.hint: V = kq/r. Electric field E = kq/r² (note: E ∝ 1/r², V ∝ 1/r).

questions.question: The capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor with area A, plate separation d and dielectric constant K is:
questions.hint: C = Kε■A/d. Dielectric constant K increases capacitance by factor K.

questions.question: Energy stored in a capacitor of capacitance C charged to voltage V is:
questions.hint: U = ½CV² = ½QV = Q²/2C. All three expressions are equivalent.

questions.question: Gauss's law: For a spherical shell of charge Q, electric field inside is:
questions.hint:
By Gauss's law, E inside a uniformly charged shell = 0 (no enclosed charge for r
< R).

questions.question: Ohm's law V = IR is valid when:
questions.hint:
Ohm's law holds when temperature (and hence resistance) is constant. R
changes with T.

questions.question: Three resistors of 2Ω, 3Ω, 6Ω are connected in parallel. Equivalent resistance is:
Kirchhoff's current law (KCL) is based on:
questions.hint:
KCL: ΣI at junction = 0. Charge cannot accumulate at a junction → conservation
of charge.

questions.question: The Wheatstone bridge is balanced when:
questions.hint:
Balance condition: P/Q = R/S. Galvanometer shows zero deflection. No current
through galvanometer.

questions.question: The power dissipated in a resistor R carrying current I is:
questions.hint: P = I²R = V²/R = VI. All are equivalent using Ohm's law V = IR.

questions.question: The force on a charge q moving with velocity v in magnetic field B is:
questions.hint: Lorentz force: F = q(v × B) = qvB sinθ. Force is perpendicular to both v and B.

questions.question: The magnetic field at the centre of a circular loop of radius R carrying current I is:
questions.hint: B = µ■I/2R at centre of circular loop. For solenoid: B = µ■nI.

questions.question: Moving coil galvanometer works on the principle of:
options: B) Torque on current loop in magnetic field
questions.explanation:
Torque on current
loop in magnetic field
questions.hint:
τ = NIAB sinθ. Current-carrying coil in B-field experiences torque → deflection
proportional to I.

questions.question: The SI unit of magnetic flux is:
questions.hint: Magnetic flux Φ = B·A. Unit = T·m² = Weber (Wb). 1 Wb = 10■ Maxwell (CGS).

questions.question: Ampere's circuital law: ■B·dl = µ■Ienc. This is analogous to Gauss's law for:
questions.hint:
Gauss's law: ■E·dA = q/ε■. Ampere's law: ■B·dl = µ■I. Both relate field to
source quantity.

questions.question: Faraday's law of EMI: The induced EMF is equal to:
options: A) Rate of change of current
B) Rate of change of magnetic flux
C) Rate of change of resistance
D) Product of B and A
questions.explanation:
Rate of change of
magnetic flux
questions.hint: e = -dΦ/dt (Faraday's law). Negative sign is Lenz's law (opposing cause).

questions.question: The rms value of AC voltage V = V■ sinωt is:
questions.hint:
V_rms = V■/√2 ≈ 0.707 V■. For I_rms = I■/√2. Mains supply: V■ = 325V,
V_rms ≈ 230V.

questions.question: At resonance in a series LCR circuit:
questions.hint:
Resonance: XL = XC → ωL = 1/ωC → ω■ = 1/√(LC). Impedance Z = R
(minimum), current is maximum.

questions.question: Power factor of a purely inductive circuit is:
questions.hint:
PF = cosφ. For pure inductor, φ = 90° (current lags voltage). PF = cos90° = 0.
No real power.

questions.question: A transformer steps voltage from 220 V to 11000 V. If primary turns = 200, secondary turns are:
The critical angle for total internal reflection for a medium of refractive index µ = √2 is:
questions.hint: sin(C) = 1/µ = 1/√2 → C = 45°. TIR occurs when angle of incidence > C.

questions.question:
Mirror formula: 1/v + 1/u = 1/f. For a concave mirror of focal length 10 cm with object at 30 cm, image
distance is:
In Young's double slit experiment, fringe width β = λD/d. If d is doubled (other things same), β:
questions.hint: β ∝ 1/d. If d → 2d, then β → β/2. Fringes become narrower.

questions.question: Polarization is a property of:
questions.hint:
Only transverse waves can be polarized. Light (transverse EM wave) can be
polarized; sound cannot.

questions.question: Resolving power of a microscope increases when:
options: A) Wavelength of light used increases
B) Wavelength of light used decreases
questions.hint:
RP = 2µsinθ/λ ∝ 1/λ. Smaller λ → better resolution. Electron microscopes use
very small λ.

questions.question:
In a single slit diffraction pattern, the central maximum is how many times wider than secondary
maxima?
questions.hint:
Central maximum width = 2λD/a; secondary maxima width = λD/a. Central is
twice as wide.

questions.question: In photoelectric effect, the stopping potential V■ is related to max KE of photoelectrons by:
questions.hint:
Stopping potential V■ just stops fastest electrons. KE_max = eV■ (energy of
electron retarded by V■).

questions.question: Bohr's model: Radius of nth orbit of hydrogen is:
questions.hint: r■ = n²a■ where a■ = 0.529 Å (Bohr radius). r■ = 0.529 Å, r■ = 4 × 0.529 Å.

questions.question: In radioactive decay, the half-life T½ and decay constant λ are related by:
questions.hint: N = N■e^(-λt). At t = T½: N = N■/2 → e^(-λT½) = 1/2 → T½ = ln2/λ = 0.693/λ

questions.question: In nuclear fission, the energy released is due to:
options: A) Increase in binding energy per nucleon
B) Decrease in binding energy per nucleon
C) Conversion of electron to positron
questions.explanation:
Increase in binding
energy per nucleon
questions.hint:
Heavy nuclei (low BE/nucleon) split into medium nuclei (high BE/nucleon).
Difference → energy (E = Δmc²).

questions.question: In α-decay, the mass number A and atomic number Z change as:
questions.hint: α particle = ■■He. In α-decay: A → A-4, Z → Z-2. E.g., ²³■U → ²³■Th + ■He

questions.question: In a p-n junction diode under forward bias:
options: A) Width of depletion layer increases
B) Width of depletion layer decreases
questions.hint:
Forward bias opposes built-in potential → depletion layer narrows → current
flows easily.

questions.question: A Zener diode is used as:
questions.hint:
Zener diode maintains constant voltage across load despite variations in input
voltage or load current.

questions.question: In a common emitter (CE) transistor amplifier, the voltage gain depends on:
options: B) β and ratio of collector to base resistance
questions.explanation:
β and ratio of
collector to base resistance
questions.hint:
Voltage gain Av = β × (Rc/Rb) in CE configuration. β = Ic/Ib (current gain).
